risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The randomisation schedule will be prepared by a researcher not involved in participant recruitment, assessment or treatment using a computer-generated random numbers table. There will be a 1:1 allocation ratio of participants to each one of the five groups. Randomisation will be conducted by random permuted blocks of same size." Comment: This explicitly describes a proper random method (computer-generated random numbers table) with permuted blocks, which is sufficient to judge the allocation sequence as truly random.

Quote: "The randomisation schedule will be prepared by a researcher not involved in participant recruitment, assessment or treatment using a computer-generated random numbers table... The list containing this information will remain with the physiotherapist responsible for the treatment. A number will be assigned to participants according to the order of their evaluation. This digit will be later located in the list and, thus, the corresponding group will be identified." Comment: Although the sequence was generated independently, the report does not describe a concealment mechanism such as central allocation, sealed opaque envelopes, or identical containers. Storing the list with the physiotherapist and assigning participants numbers by order of evaluation does not clearly prevent the recruiter from foreseeing the next assignment, so allocation concealment cannot be judged low risk.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "This study will be a five-arm randomised controlled trial with blind evaluator." Comment: Only the evaluator is stated to be blinded; participants and the treating physiotherapist are not described as blinded. The interventions are visibly different (active laser vs placebo laser vs stretching vs education), and the main outcomes include pain and function (VAS, WOMAC, Lequesne, TUG), which could plausibly be influenced by participants’ and personnel’s knowledge of allocation. Therefore, performance bias is likely high overall.

Quote: "The assessor will perform the assessment unaware of the group to which each participant will belong." Comment: Outcome assessment was done by a blinded assessor, and the main outcomes in the review context were patient-reported pain (VAS) and function (Lequesne index). Although these are subjective outcomes, the reported blinding of the assessor indicates low risk of detection bias for the assessed outcomes. The questionnaire was also administered by the assessor rather than self-scored, further supporting blinded outcome assessment.

Summary: The protocol states that the trial aimed to include 29 participants per group to allow for a 15% drop-out rate, and the timeline says "All participants completed the study in December of 2014." The review characteristics also describe the interventions as delivered entirely in-person over 8 weeks, with no signal of differential loss to follow-up or missing outcome data. Comment: Although the paper is a protocol and does not report per-arm attrition numbers, it communicates essentially complete follow-up at the planned study end and no evidence of imbalanced or outcome-related missing data, so attrition bias is unlikely.
